title: Panic Catastrophic Thought Challenger
description: Use this worksheet to identify and challenge the catastrophic thoughts that fuel panic attacks. This is cognitive restructuring applied directly to panic.
instructions: When you notice panic-related anxiety rising — or after a panic attack — work through each step below. Start with the triggering sensation, identify the catastrophic thought, examine the evidence, and develop a more balanced interpretation.

fields.label: 1. Trigger — What sensation or situation started the panic?
fields.placeholder: e.g., I noticed my heart beating fast while sitting at my desk...

fields.label: 2. Catastrophic Thought — What did you think was happening?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'I'm having a heart attack', 'I'm going to faint', 'I'm losing my mind'

fields.label: 3. Fear Intensity — How scared were you? (0 = not at all, 100 = most terrified ever)
4. Thinking Trap — Which cognitive distortion best fits?

options.label: Catastrophizing — Jumping to the worst possible outcome
Emotional Reasoning — 'I feel scared so something must be dangerous'
Fortune Telling — Predicting a negative outcome
Interoceptive Bias — Over-focusing on body sensations
Mind Reading — 'Everyone can see something is wrong with me'

fields.label: 5. Evidence For — What makes you believe the catastrophic thought?
fields.placeholder: e.g., My heart was racing, I felt dizzy...

fields.label: 6. Evidence Against — What contradicts the catastrophic thought?
fields.placeholder: e.g., I've had this sensation 50+ times and it was always panic, not a heart attack. My doctor said my heart is healthy. The sensation passed in 15 minutes.

fields.label: 7. Balanced Thought — What is a more accurate interpretation?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'My heart is racing because of adrenaline, not because something is wrong. This is a panic attack and it will pass on its own.'

fields.label: 8. Re-rate Fear — How scared do you feel now? (0 = not at all, 100 = most terrified ever)

distortionsList.name: Catastrophizing
distortionsList.description: Jumping to the worst possible outcome: 'My heart is racing so I must be having a heart attack.'

distortionsList.name: Emotional Reasoning
distortionsList.description: Believing feelings are facts: 'I feel terrified, so something terrible must be happening.'

distortionsList.name: Fortune Telling
distortionsList.description: Predicting negative outcomes: 'I know I'm going to have a panic attack at the party.'

distortionsList.name: Interoceptive Bias
distortionsList.description: Selectively noticing and amplifying body sensations: 'My heart skipped a beat — something is wrong.'

distortionsList.name: Mind Reading
distortionsList.description: Assuming others can see your distress: 'Everyone can tell I'm about to lose control.'

distortionsList.name: Overgeneralization
distortionsList.description: Drawing broad conclusions from single events: 'I panicked once at the store, so I can never go back.'

disclaimer: This worksheet is for educational purposes only and is not a substitute for professional mental health treatment. If you are in crisis, contact the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline by calling or texting 988.
